text: I wrote this article using an AI. Since the emergence of ChatGPT, there has been a lot of discussion in academia whether using GPT-3.5 in our work is a good idea or not. While some suggest it will make our lives easier and our writing better, others share concerns about potential plagiarism, the spread of misinformation and the enormous environmental impact of running large applications. Furthermore, there is a growing number of publications about being transparent and using AI in a “smart” manner, but very few publications have actually tested whether the usage of AI is beneficial or not in an academic context. This article is therefore a result of a participatory experiment, where I used OpenAI’s *text-davinci-003* model to write the article itself. I tested the model to act as an AI in five different roles within my workflow as an academic, namely: - Chunk Stylist - Bullet-to-Paragraph - Talk Textualizer - Research Buddy - Polisher I will present my findings about these roles, including some thoughts about the underlying implications for research integrity. The full dataset of all prompts I sent to the AI, the outputs it gave me and the final edits I needed to do to sound human are included. They also contain information about the percentage of changes needed for every single output, in order to present an in-depth analysis of the actual amount of work needed to adjust the outputs to sound natural and human.
explanation: The passage reads as a reflective, first-person academic discussion with specific methodology and personal context, which is typical of human authorship. It does not display obvious AI-generated artifacts like repetitive phrasing or generic content.
